CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-18-2018 Continued To Dec-05-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Action Pending Arbitration
Off Calendar Jan-22-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Andrew M. Massara of the law offices of Long & Levit, LLP (415-397-2222) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

No other appearance is made in this matter

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT BPN-RPC STAFFING, INC.'S Motion To Stay Action Pending Arbitration is GRANTED. No opposition filed. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Newton Lam: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, e-mail: mtorreano@sftc.org (L&M302)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-17-2017 Continued To Jun-06-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Case
Off Calendar Sep-07-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT BPN-RPC STAFFING, INC.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Case as follows: Defendant BPN-RPC Staffing, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration is granted as to the first through third causes of action and is granted as to the fourth cause of action to the extent plaintiff Jason Stirling seeks victim-specific relief (i.e. lost wages and work benefits per Labor Code 6310(b)) but denied to the extent Mr. Stirling seeks civil penalties). While the parties' arbitration agreement is both procedurally and substantively unconscionable, the sole substantively unconscionable provision which gives the arbitrator the "final authority" to award reimbursement of initial fees to SPARC can and will be severed. Mr. Stirling has shown there was some procedural unconscionability. Per Mr. Stirling's declaration, he was told by a representative of SPARC that he had to sign the arbitration agreement to be employed. SPARC's failure to provide the applicable arbitration rules to Mr. Stirling is not grounds for a finding of any unconscionability because Mr. Stirling has not challenged any provision of those rules. (Baltazar v. Forever, 21, Inc. (2016) 62 Cal.4th 1237, 1246.) The agreement is substantively unconscionable because it exposes Mr. Stirling to the risk having to pay all of the costs of initiating the arbitration, which exceed what he would have to pay if he brought a civil action in court. "The arbitration agreement . . . cannot generally require the employee to bear any type of expense that the employee would not be required to bear if her or she were free to bring the action in court." (McManus v. CIBC World Markets Corp. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 76, 93 (internal quotations omitted).) The arbitration agreement does not lack mutuality. The absence of a signature by SPARC does not show that mutuality was absent. By its conduct in presenting the agreement to Mr. Stirling, SPARC showed its intent to be bound by the agreement. Nor does the sentence "By agreeing to arbitrate employment disputes, you give up the right to a trial in court" show lack of mutuality given the unambiguous language in the agreement that "the parties [including SPARC] ' agree to arbitrate all disputes'" Because there is only one provision that is substantively unconscionable and that provision is not the central purpose of the contract, the reimbursement of the initial fee provision is severed. Per Esparza v. KS Industries, L.P. (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 1228, the portion of Mr. Stirling's PAGA claim seeking victim-specific relief is subject to arbitration, while the portion of that claim seeking civil penalties is not. The court does not address SPARC's request, made for the first time in its reply memorandum, that the portion of Mr. Stirling's PAGA claim is not subject to arbitration should be stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration. [The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.] Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Reporter: Nicole Rodich, CSR#11604, nrodich@sftc.org.
